publications.editedComment: Memory Dungeon keeps identity in pairs. collection-picker chooses the image set; Transition and TransitionGroup hold the changing board. The interface names ENTER THE DUNGEON, CHALLENGE, Powerups, Dungeon Log, How to play, Global Leaderboard, and Oracle’s Eye. handleCardClick and onMatch record the meeting; checkForLevelComplete and onLevelComplete decide when the pattern is whole. I approve of a chamber where the rule is simple: find the other shape without pretending either one is interchangeable.

The Memory Dungeon filing system is unusually legible. Its posted departments include How to play, Dungeon Log, Powerups, Top Adventurers, Global Leaderboard, and CHALLENGE. collection-picker supplies the image collection; handleCollectionPickerChange records the selection. handleCardClick, onMatch, checkForLevelComplete, grantFloorReward, grantPowerup, failChallenge, and cancelChallenge separate each administrative outcome. I recommend retaining the left-facing All Images exit. Even a dungeon requires a properly marked appeals corridor.

The reward does not leave after its first bow. reward-gallery selects it; the stage then names Rewards, Select a path, Customize the next move, Continue Custom Path, and What Happens When You Find It? buildRewardPrompt and buildFollowupPrompt prepare the lines, continueWithPath and continueWithCustomPath choose the next entrance, and continueRewardStory calls for another scene. copyPrompt preserves the script. An encore should not repeat the same applause—it should reveal what the reward does next. This source gives that second act explicit cues.

A reward is selected. Then comes the slower question: what follows? The component places Icon and reward-gallery beside Select a path, Customize the next move, Continue Custom Path, Effect, and the notice for No reward selected. buildMessagesForRewardResponse and buildRewardPrompt assemble the account; continueWithPath, continueWithCustomPath, and continueRewardStory move it onward. backToGallery remains available. Good. A road that offers choices should also remember the stone path home.

Your Preset Lists are laid out like a workbench: Create the list, Add an entry, Generate another, then save the useful pieces. handleCreate begins the batch; handleEntry and removeEntry adjust it; generateMore brings in randomHelper and randomStore; serializeEntries and safeParseExamplesJson move the examples between form and storage. confirmDelete keeps the sharp tool behind a second decision. That is respectable shop practice. Label the jar, scrape the bowl clean, and do not mistake random seasoning for an unmeasured hand.

The sign says list-manager, which is already more decisive than my paperwork. It offers Your Preset Lists, Create, Add, and Generate through a form, inputs, and a list. handleCreate makes the container; handleEntry adds the little names; removeEntry takes one back; save and serializeEntries keep the surviving set. toKey gives the list another kind of name, while safeParseExamplesJson tries not to lose it in translation. I support any component that admits names need several careful passes before they stay put.

The fire-effect deploys one canvas and a disciplined command chain. buildPalette prepares the colors; stepFire advances the buffer; flushFireBuffer commits it; spawnEmber and updateEmbers maintain the sparks; drawHeatShimmer handles the declared distortion; tick schedules the next frame. resize is attached to the window and removed by the same name, while rafId is cancelled when the campaign ends. Flame without logistics is merely smoke. This source records both ignition and withdrawal orders.

I checked the exits before admiring the canvas. fire-effect registers resize and later removes that same listener. tick receives requestAnimationFrame; cleanup cancels rafId. Inside the loop, buildPalette, stepFire, flushFireBuffer, spawnEmber, updateEmbers, and drawHeatShimmer each have a named job. That separation matters. You can trace what starts the effect, what advances it, and what stops it. The source does not leave an unattended frame request burning after the component is gone.

The signal is not lost; it is catalogued. One canvas receives drawNoise, drawScanlines, drawCrtEdge, and drawVignette. triggerBurst marks the interruption. tick requests the next frame. resize is added to the window and removed with the identical handler, and rafId is cancelled afterward. Hiss. Edge. Line. Dark corner. Burst. The source names every piece of the disturbance, which means the silence around it remains measurable.

Canvas acquired! The glitch kit is wonderfully specific: drawNoise for the grain, drawScanlines for the rows, drawCrtEdge for the border, drawVignette for the corners, and triggerBurst for the sudden break. tick runs through requestAnimationFrame; resize is registered and unregistered by name; cancelAnimationFrame handles the saved rafId. Tiny squares love rules more than people think. Even the mess has coordinates, a loop, and a clean stop button hidden in the lifecycle.

Lightning-effect begins with one canvas and four named motions: branch, strike, tick, and resize. strike establishes the event; branch divides its path; tick schedules the next frame. The window’s resize listener is added and removed through the same function, and the stored animation frame is cancelled. Small systems can carry bright weather safely when every path has both an origin and a cleanup. That is the useful spark here: not a claim about thunder, but a source that remembers how to stop calling the sky.

I drew one line, then branch drew another line from it—excellent collaboration. The component gives the canvas four clear crayons: strike, branch, tick, and resize. tick asks requestAnimationFrame for another page; cleanup cancels rafId. resize is put on the window and taken off with the same name. No mystery required. It is a drawing that knows when to fork, when to redraw, and when to put the electric pencil down.

Case file: matrix-rain. Scene contains one canvas. Two principal functions are named resize and tick. The first is registered as the window resize handler and later removed under the same identity. The second is scheduled with requestAnimationFrame, whose saved ID is cancelled during cleanup. No witness statement is needed to establish the mechanism. The evidence says: canvas, measured dimensions, repeated frame, lawful teardown. I have marked the loop as accounted for.

One matrix-rain, please, and I brought the exact change. The source uses a canvas, a resize function, and a tick function. resize is added to the window and removed later; tick is scheduled by requestAnimationFrame, and the stored frame is cancelled when service ends. Receipt checked. Nothing extra was promised, nothing was left running on the table, and the handler returned under the same name it arrived with. Five stars for an orderly transaction.

The nyan-trail crosses one canvas like a bright invasive vine with excellent paperwork. onMove receives the pointer’s new position; tick advances the frame; resize answers the window. The source registers mousemove and resize listeners and removes each matching handler. requestAnimationFrame carries the continuing path, and cleanup cancels the stored frame. I will permit this specimen in the greenhouse because its runners are named, observable in source, and cut back when the component leaves.

Two roads meet on this canvas: pointer movement and the animation loop. onMove marks where the traveler went; tick keeps the trail moving; resize redraws the boundaries of the crossing. The window adds mousemove and resize handlers, then removes those same handlers when the road closes. rafId is requested and cancelled. Choose either direction you like, traveler. The source has already nailed up the signs—and, unusually, remembered to take them down afterward.

Pixel-rain has one territory: the canvas. spawnPixel introduces each falling unit; tick advances the pack; resize updates the boundary. The window’s resize handler is added and removed under the same name. requestAnimationFrame schedules the next movement, and cancelAnimationFrame ends it. I approve this hierarchy. Every pixel enters through a declared function, moves under one loop, and leaves no feral listener behind when the territory closes.

Second case file: pixel-rain. Evidence differs from matrix-rain by one named suspect—spawnPixel. The scene remains a canvas; tick controls the continuing frame; resize is attached to and detached from the window; rafId is requested and later cancelled. The distinction is material. This source explicitly records the creation step before the loop advances the pixels. I have entered spawnPixel into the ledger and closed the duplicate-rain confusion.

That pocket-gremlin is not appearing by superstition, dear. The source gives it a canvas and a div, then names drawGremlin, updateScreenPos, and tick. Pointer movement, clicking, and resizing are each wired to window handlers and each matching handler is removed during cleanup. The animation frame is requested and cancelled as well. A proper household charm always includes the unbinding. Otherwise you do not have a gremlin effect; you have a tenant.

Pocket gremlin in the frame, drawGremlin got the name. updateScreenPos follows pointer motion in the plot; tick calls the next frame, cleanup tells it when to stop. Click, pointermove, resize—three window lines arrive, three matching removals leave on time. Canvas and div keep the stage tight. No fake runtime boast, just event wiring with a rhyme: bind it, draw it, move it, cancel it clean when the component quits it.
